edges.description: Loss of neurofibromin removes its RAS-GTPase-activating function, de-repressing RAS in the neoplastic Schwann cell lineage.
edges.source: Biallelic NF1 Inactivation in Schwann Cell Lineage

edges.description: NF1 loss alters Schwann cell differentiation in addition to its effect on RAS-driven growth.
edges.source: Biallelic NF1 Inactivation in Schwann Cell Lineage

edges.description: Active RAS-GTP drives the RAF-MEK-ERK cascade, the principal proliferative output downstream of NF1 loss and the target of MEK inhibitor therapy.
MAPK-driven growth signaling promotes proliferation of the neoplastic Schwann cell lineage.
edges.target: Schwann Cell Proliferation and Progenitor Expansion

edges.description: The expanded stem-like progenitor pool provides the cells of origin that proliferate to form the tumor.
edges.target: Schwann Cell Proliferation and Progenitor Expansion

edges.description: The expanding NF1-null Schwann cell population recruits and reprograms immune and stromal cells, which come to dominate the tumor mass.
edges.source: Schwann Cell Proliferation and Progenitor Expansion

edges.description: Sustained proliferation of the neoplastic Schwann cell lineage forms the localized (cutaneous, subcutaneous, spinal) neurofibroma.
edges.source: Schwann Cell Proliferation and Progenitor Expansion

edges.description: When initiated in Schwann lineage cells along large nerves and plexuses, the proliferating tumor forms a plexiform neurofibroma.
edges.source: Schwann Cell Proliferation and Progenitor Expansion

edges.description: A subset of plexiform neurofibromas accumulate additional somatic lesions and progress along the pathway to malignant transformation.
edges.source: Schwann Cell Proliferation and Progenitor Expansion

edges.description: Tumor mass, nerve involvement, and the inflammatory microenvironment contribute to neurofibroma-associated pain.
Tumor mass effect and peripheral nerve compression/involvement produce motor dysfunction and muscle weakness.
Sequential CDKN2A and PRC2 loss in a plexiform neurofibroma drives transformation to malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor.

edges.target: Biallelic NF1 Inactivation in Schwann Cell Lineage

nodes.description: Neurofibroma initiation requires loss of both NF1 alleles in a Schwann cell lineage progenitor. In NF1 patients, one germline loss-of-function NF1 variant is inherited and a somatic second hit (loss of heterozygosity) inactivates the remaining wild-type allele, following the classic Knudson two-hit model. Sporadic solitary neurofibromas arise from two somatic NF1 hits in the absence of a germline variant.
nodes.id: Biallelic NF1 Inactivation in Schwann Cell Lineage

evidence.snippet: NF1-null SLCs formed bona fide neurofibromas with high levels of SOX10 expression

nodes.description: Sustained RAS-GTP signaling constitutively activates the RAF-MEK-ERK (MAPK) cascade, the dominant proliferative signal in NF1-deficient Schwann cells and the molecular target of MEK1/2 inhibitors such as selumetinib.

evidence.snippet: in addition to regulating MAPK-mediated cell growth

nodes.description: Beyond its effect on RAS-MAPK growth signaling, NF1 loss impairs Schwann cell differentiation, inducing a persistent stem-like state that expands the pool of SOX10+ progenitors competent to initiate a neurofibroma.

evidence.snippet: NF1 loss impaired Schwann cell differentiation by inducing a persistent stem-like state to expand the pool of progenitors required to initiate tumor formation

nodes.description: Malignant transformation of a plexiform neurofibroma to an aggressive soft-tissue sarcoma (neurofibrosarcoma / MPNST).

evidence.snippet: Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST) is an aggressive soft tissue sarcoma with limited therapeutic options and a poor prognosis.

nodes.description: Plexiform neurofibromas are precursor lesions for malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor. Transformation follows a stepwise molecular cascade: on the background of biallelic NF1 loss, inactivation of CDKN2A/B yields an atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of uncertain biological potential (ANNUBP), and subsequent disruption of the polycomb repressive complex 2 (PRC2; SUZ12/EED) with loss of H3K27me3 drives progression to high-grade MPNST.

evidence.snippet: Plexiform neurofibroma (PN) and atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of unknown biological potential (ANNUBP) are novel concepts of MPNST precancerous lesions, which revealed sequential mutations in MPNST development.

nodes.description: Oral MEK inhibitor for NF1-associated symptomatic plexiform neurofibromas, with reported partial responses and improvements in pain and function; acneiform rash is a common class toxicity.

evidence.snippet: Recent advancements in targeted therapies, particularly MEK inhibitors, have introduced promising treatment options for patients with severe manifestations of NF1.

nodes.description: Motor dysfunction (weakness and reduced strength/range of motion) from tumor mass effect and nerve involvement is a frequent plexiform neurofibroma complication; SPRINT measured strength improvement with selumetinib.

evidence.snippet: The most frequent neurofibroma-related symptoms were disfigurement (44 patients), motor dysfunction (33), and pain (26).

meta.association: Germline predisposition with biallelic tumor-suppressor loss

evidence.snippet: Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) is an autosomal dominant genetic disease and one of the most common inherited tumor predisposition syndromes, affecting 1 in 3000 individuals worldwide.

nodes.description: Benign peripheral nerve sheath tumor of the Schwann cell lineage, presenting most commonly as cutaneous, subcutaneous, or spinal lesions.

evidence.snippet: affected patients develop Schwann cell lineage peripheral nerve sheath tumors (neurofibromas)

nodes.description: Neurofibroma-related pain is a frequent and clinically significant symptom, particularly with plexiform neurofibromas, and is a primary driver of treatment.

evidence.snippet: The most frequent neurofibroma-related symptoms were disfigurement (44 patients), motor dysfunction (33), and pain (26).

nodes.description: Neurofibroma arising along multiple fascicles of large nerves and plexuses, frequently congenital, and a precursor to MPNST.

evidence.snippet: Plexiform neurofibroma (PN) and atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of unknown biological potential (ANNUBP) are novel concepts of MPNST precancerous lesions

nodes.description: Neurofibromin is a RAS-GTPase-activating protein (RAS-GAP) that normally converts active RAS-GTP to inactive RAS-GDP. Its loss leaves RAS in the active GTP-bound state, producing constitutive RAS signal transduction that feeds the downstream RAF-MEK-ERK (MAPK) and PI3K-AKT-mTOR effector cascades.

evidence.snippet: The NF1 gene encodes neurofibromin, a large protein with RAS GTP-ase activating (RAS-GAP) activity, and loss of NF1 results in increased RAS signaling.

nodes.description: Proliferation of NF1-null Schwann cells and SOX10+ progenitor-like cells generates the neoplastic compartment of the neurofibroma. Inactivation of both Nf1 alleles in Sox10+ cells is sufficient to generate classic nodular cutaneous and plexiform neurofibromas in vivo.
nodes.id: Schwann Cell Proliferation and Progenitor Expansion

evidence.snippet: both Nf1 alleles were inactivated in mouse Sox10+ cells, leading to classic nodular cutaneous and plexiform neurofibroma formation that completely recapitulated their human counterparts

nodes.description: Oral MEK1/2 inhibitor that suppresses the RAF-MEK-ERK signaling driven by NF1 loss. FDA-approved (2020) for pediatric patients with symptomatic, inoperable NF1-associated plexiform neurofibromas; the pivotal phase 2 SPRINT trial showed durable partial responses with pain and quality-of-life benefit.

evidence.snippet: A total of 35 patients (70%) had a confirmed partial response as of March 29, 2019, and 28 of these patients had a durable response (lasting ≥1 year).

nodes.description: Long after Nf1 loss, neurofibromas recruit and reprogram a complex microenvironment: in human plexiform neurofibromas immune and stromal cells comprise roughly 90% of the tumor, with monocyte recruitment, aberrant macrophage differentiation, fibroblast-driven collagen deposition, and deregulated cell-cell communication (e.g., MIF-CD74/NF-kB). This microenvironment supports tumor growth and immunosuppression and contributes to mass effect, pain, and disfigurement.

evidence.snippet: showed dramatic expansion of immune and stromal cell populations; in corresponding human PNs, the immune and stromal cells comprised 90% of cells
